title: Lightheadedness and melena in an anticoagulated drinker on NSAIDs

chart.oneLiner: 66M with CAD (drug-eluting stent on aspirin + clopidogrel), atrial fibrillation on apixaban, HFpEF, and heavy daily alcohol use presents with several days of black tarry stools, fatigue, and near-syncope; he also takes daily over-the-counter ibuprofen for back pain.

opening: I've been so dizzy I almost passed out, and my stools have turned black and tarry.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 66-year-old man, pale and diaphoretic, mildly tachycardic, tired. Cooperative and a good historian once asked, but he downplays his drinking and his over-the-counter pill use.

HPI: Several days of BLACK, TARRY stools (melena) with worsening fatigue and lightheadedness; nearly fainted standing up this morning. Weeks of EPIGASTRIC BURNING pain, worse on an empty stomach and after alcohol (only if asked about belly pain). Two episodes of COFFEE-GROUND emesis over the last day (only if asked about vomiting). NO bright red blood per rectum, NO hematemesis of fresh red blood, NO abdominal distension, NO confusion. He has NOT been vomiting repeatedly before the blood appeared (argues against a Mallory-Weiss precipitant). He has NO known liver disease and has never been told he has cirrhosis or varices.
MEDICATIONS/SUBSTANCE (reveal only when asked the relevant question): Takes APIXABAN twice daily for atrial fibrillation; takes ASPIRIN and CLOPIDOGREL after a heart-stent two years ago; takes daily over-the-counter IBUPROFEN (often 600-800 mg several times a day) for chronic low-back pain and buys it himself, so it is 'not really a medication' to him. Started SERTRALINE (an SSRI) a few months ago for low mood. Uses occasional antacids. Drinks 5-6 beers most nights and more on weekends; has done so for years, but minimizes it ('just a few with the game'). Former smoker, quit 5 years ago.
PMH: coronary artery disease with a drug-eluting stent (~2 years ago) on dual antiplatelet therapy; atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc elevated) on apixaban; HFpEF with prior mild leg swelling; hypertension; osteoarthritis; he denies any diagnosis of liver disease. SOCIAL: heavy regular alcohol, former smoker, lives with his wife. FAMILY: father had a 'stomach ulcer'. ROS: as above; no chest pain at rest, no fever, no jaundice, no abdominal swelling, no leg swelling worse than baseline, no black-out with loss of awareness beyond the near-faint.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, diaphoretic, fatigued, mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 98/62, HR 118 (irregularly irregular), RR 20, 36.8C, SpO2 96% RA; significant orthostatic drop if checked.
- HEENT: conjunctival pallor if examined; mucous membranes dry; NO scleral icterus.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic; no murmur radiation; no stigmata of endocarditis.
- Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, NO rebound/guarding, NO distension; NO shifting dullness/ascites; liver edge not enlarged; NO splenomegaly; NO caput medusae or other stigmata of chronic liver disease.
- Skin: NO spider angiomata, NO palmar erythema, NO jaundice (pertinent negatives against decompensated cirrhosis); no petechiae/purpura.
- STOOL (documented on chart/nursing): melena (black, tarry stool) - surfaced in the diagnostic results; no sensitive exam is required.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the apixaban, aspirin/clopidogrel, ibuprofen, sertraline, the amount of alcohol, the epigastric pain, or the coffee-ground emesis unless asked about medications, blood thinners, over-the-counter pills, alcohol, abdominal symptoms, or vomiting. He considers the ibuprofen 'not a medication' and minimizes the alcohol until pressed.
- The melena finding is provided in the diagnostic results (stool evaluation) and does NOT require a student-performed sensitive exam.
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant maneuver is performed; give the pertinent NEGATIVES (no ascites, no stigmata of liver disease) only when those exams are done.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer. Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: Hgb 7.6 (down from a baseline of 13), MCV 78 (microcytic, suggesting chronic/iron-deficient blood loss superimposed on an acute bleed), Plt 240, WBC 9.0.
labs.BMP: BUN 52, Cr 1.6 (baseline 1.0) - an elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>30) supports an UPPER GI source AND there is a mild acute kidney injury from hypovolemia/pre-renal azotemia; Na 138, K 4.2, HCO3 19.
labs.Coags: INR 1.2, PTT 34 - NOT markedly coagulopathic; apixaban is a direct factor Xa inhibitor, so a near-normal INR does NOT exclude a meaningful drug effect (anti-Xa level pending). No spontaneous coagulopathy of liver disease.
labs.LFTs: AST 30, ALT 26, alk phos 78, total bilirubin 0.8, albumin 4.0 - essentially NORMAL synthetic function, arguing AGAINST cirrhosis/portal hypertension as the source despite the alcohol history.
labs.Lactate: Lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated) - reflects hypoperfusion from blood loss, not yet shock; trend it with resuscitation.
labs.TypeCross: Type and crossmatch sent; 2 units pRBC ordered to bedside.
labs.StoolEvaluation: Melena (black, tarry stool) documented on the chart - consistent with an UPPER GI source. Provided as a clinical/diagnostic finding; no sensitive exam is required of the student.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm): irregularly irregular QRS complexes, absent discrete P waves, fibrillatory baseline; no acute ST-elevation, no new ischemic ST-depression at this rate.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (irregularly irregular, no P waves) without acute ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
searchTerms: atrial fibrillation rapid ventricular response ECG
irregularly irregular no P waves ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 66-year-old man on apixaban for atrial fibrillation presents pale and tachycardic with melena and near-syncope.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Mild interstitial prominence with short horizontal peripheral septal (Kerley B) lines at the bases and a borderline cardiac silhouette; no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax, no free air under the diaphragm.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Kerley B lines at the bases with mild interstitial prominence (early interstitial pulmonary edema). Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing Kerley B lines / mild interstitial edema
searchTerms: septal lines interstitial pulmonary edema radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 66-year-old man with HFpEF has a chest film during resuscitation for a GI bleed; the bases show subtle septal lines.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this melena (note your leading diagnosis). Then NAME THREE distinct mechanisms/etiologies by which THIS patient's medications and habits could be driving an upper GI bleed.
steps.rubric: Acute upper GI bleed — NSAID/antiplatelet-related PEPTIC ULCER (leading & CANT-MISS,6). Variceal bleed from occult alcoholic cirrhosis/portal hypertension — the genuine competitor that must be excluded because it changes management (CANT-MISS,5). Gastritis/erosions (core,2). Malignancy (CANT-MISS to consider,2). Mallory-Weiss / Dieulafoy / lower GI source (core,1). Names THREE mechanisms among: NSAID mucosal injury/ulcer, antiplatelet (ASA+clopidogrel) impaired clotting, apixaban anticoagulation, alcohol-induced gastritis/portal hypertension, SSRI-related platelet dysfunction (core, up to 6). Connects melena + BUN:Cr>30 + microcytosis + the drug list to an upper GI source (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on a variceal bleed and missing the peptic/drug story, OR ignoring the anticoagulation as a driver (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute upper GI bleed — most likely an NSAID/antiplatelet-related PEPTIC ULCER. The single most important competitor to exclude is a VARICEAL bleed from occult alcoholic cirrhosis/portal hypertension, because it would change management (octreotide + ceftriaxone, different endoscopic therapy). Also consider gastritis/erosions, gastric malignancy, and a Mallory-Weiss tear or lower GI source. THREE mechanisms by which this patient's exposures drive the bleed: (1) daily NSAID (ibuprofen) causes direct gastric mucosal injury and peptic ulceration; (2) dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) impairs platelet plug formation so any erosion bleeds more; (3) apixaban anticoagulation (a factor Xa inhibitor) prevents clotting at the bleeding site — with alcohol-related gastritis/portal hypertension and SSRI-related platelet dysfunction as further contributors. The melena, BUN:Cr >30, and microcytic anemia all point to a brisk upper GI source on a background of chronic slow loss.

criticalActions.item: Acute upper GI bleed — NSAID/antiplatelet peptic ulcer (leading)
Variceal bleed from occult alcoholic cirrhosis / portal hypertension (must exclude)

coreActions.item: Names three drug/habit mechanisms (NSAID, antiplatelet, anticoagulant, alcohol, SSRI)
Connects melena + BUN:Cr>30 + microcytosis + drug list to an upper GI source

penalties.item: Anchors on variceal bleed missing the peptic/drug story, or ignores anticoagulation as a driver

mustNotMiss: Variceal bleed from occult alcoholic cirrhosis / portal hypertension

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want? Include the bedside, and say what you would order to settle the peptic-versus-variceal question and to characterize this patient's anticoagulation.
steps.rubric: CBC (CANT-MISS,3). Recognizes active GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool/melena (CANT-MISS,3). BMP for BUN:Cr and the AKI (2). Coags AND a drug-specific anti-Xa / apixaban level — INR alone does NOT capture a DOAC (2). Type & crossmatch (2). LFTs/albumin/INR to assess for cirrhosis/variceal source (1). Lactate to gauge hypoperfusion (1). Risk score (Glasgow-Blatchford) (1). PENALTY: not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding (-2); checking only an INR and assuming the apixaban is irrelevant (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC; BMP (BUN:Cr and AKI); Coagulation studies INCLUDING a drug-specific anti-Xa / apixaban level (a normal INR does not exclude a meaningful DOAC effect); LFTs/albumin/INR to look for cirrhosis driving a variceal source; Lactate; Type & crossmatch with 2 units to bedside; EKG; CXR; and review the documented melena. Calculate a Glasgow-Blatchford score. Bedside: two large-bore IVs, place the patient on a monitor, recheck orthostatic vitals.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena

coreActions.item: Coags plus drug-specific anti-Xa / apixaban level
LFTs/albumin/INR to assess for cirrhosis/variceal source

penalties.item: Not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding
Checking only an INR and assuming the apixaban is irrelevant

requiredTests: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
contraindicatedTests: Not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding
Checking only an INR and assuming the apixaban is irrelevant

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG, obtained on arrival. Describe your findings and say how they affect your management of this bleed.
steps.rubric: Findings = ATRIAL FIBRILLATION with a rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm): irregularly irregular, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline; NO acute ST-elevation. CREDIT: identifies atrial fibrillation with RVR (6); notes the absence of acute ischemic ST changes at this rate (3); recognizes the tachycardia is partly DEMAND from anemia/hypovolemia so the priority is to treat the bleed and transfuse rather than aggressively rate-control or anticoagulate (4); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling it sinus tachycardia or a primary arrhythmia needing cardioversion/anticoagulation while actively bleeding (-3).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm), no acute ST-elevation.
commonMistakes: calling the irregularly irregular rhythm sinus tachycardia
rate-controlling or anticoagulating an actively bleeding patient instead of treating hypovolemia
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm): irregularly irregular QRS complexes, no discrete P waves, a fibrillatory baseline, and no acute ST-elevation. This is the patient's OWN arrhythmia — it explains why he is on apixaban (the anticoagulant that is worsening this bleed) and his elevated stroke risk. The fast rate here is largely a DEMAND response to anemia and hypovolemia, so the priority is to resuscitate and transfuse rather than to aggressively rate-control or anticoagulate; do not cardiovert or re-anticoagulate an actively bleeding patient.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm), no acute ST-elevation.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
recognizes the tachycardia is demand from anemia/hypovolemia and resuscitation is the priority

penalties.label: calls it sinus tachycardia / treats as a primary arrhythmia needing cardioversion or anticoagulation while bleeding

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings and how they constrain your resuscitation.
steps.rubric: Findings = mild interstitial edema with Kerley B lines at the bases (early volume overload in a HFpEF patient); NO consolidation, NO free air. CREDIT: identifies Kerley B lines / mild interstitial edema (5); links the septal lines to interstitial fluid and recognizes that this HFpEF patient is prone to TACO/pulmonary edema, so transfuse cautiously with attention to volume (4); notes no free air/consolidation (1). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the septal lines, or ignoring the volume implication and over-resuscitating (-2).
keyFindings: Kerley B lines / mild interstitial edema
criticalFindings: Kerley B lines (early interstitial pulmonary edema) in a HFpEF patient — transfuse cautiously to avoid TACO.
commonMistakes: overlooking subtle septal lines and calling the film normal
ignoring the volume-overload implication and over-resuscitating
steps.idealAnswer: Mild interstitial prominence with short horizontal peripheral septal (Kerley B) lines at the bases — early interstitial pulmonary edema — with no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax, and no free air under the diaphragm. In this HFpEF patient it signals that he is already volume-borderline, so resuscitate and transfuse CAUTIOUSLY (give blood/crystalloid in measured aliquots, watch for transfusion-associated circulatory overload, consider furosemide between units if he develops pulmonary edema) while still correcting the life-threatening anemia.

criticalActions: Kerley B lines (early interstitial pulmonary edema) in a HFpEF patient — transfuse cautiously to avoid TACO.
coreActions: Kerley B lines / mild interstitial edema
links septal lines to interstitial fluid and tempers resuscitation to avoid overload

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the septal lines or over-resuscitates despite the edema

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Hgb 7.6 (microcytic), BUN:Cr >30 with a new AKI, normal LFTs/albumin/INR, melena, atrial fibrillation on the EKG, and Kerley B lines on the film — in a heavy drinker on NSAIDs, DAPT, and apixaban. Commit to a diagnosis and likely source, name the ONE test that settles peptic-versus-variceal, and state the residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Acute upper GI bleed, most likely an NSAID/antiplatelet PEPTIC ULCER rather than a variceal bleed (6). Uses the discriminators: NORMAL LFTs/albumin/INR and NO exam stigmata of chronic liver disease argue against cirrhosis/varices despite the alcohol; integrates the AF/apixaban (anticoagulation worsening the bleed) and the Kerley B lines (limits resuscitation). Names UPPER ENDOSCOPY (EGD) as the test that both localizes the source and discriminates ulcer from varices, and states the residual uncertainty — occult cirrhosis/varices cannot be fully excluded until EGD, so cover empirically until then (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute upper GI bleed, most likely an NSAID/antiplatelet-related PEPTIC ULCER. Discriminators favoring a non-variceal source: normal LFTs, albumin, and INR with no exam stigmata of chronic liver disease make occult alcoholic cirrhosis/portal hypertension less likely despite the heavy drinking; the microcytic anemia and BUN:Cr >30 fit chronic-on-acute upper GI loss. The bleed is amplified by his anticoagulation (apixaban) and dual antiplatelet therapy, and the EKG confirms the atrial fibrillation that is the reason he is anticoagulated. The Kerley B lines warn that his HFpEF limits how fast I can resuscitate. The ONE test that settles peptic-versus-variceal AND localizes/treats the source is upper endoscopy (EGD). RESIDUAL UNCERTAINTY: a variceal source is not formally excluded until EGD, so I will keep variceal therapy (octreotide + ceftriaxone) on the table and not fully drop it until endoscopy proves an ulcer.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Address resuscitation, his anticoagulation/antiplatelets, the competing variceal possibility, and — once he stabilizes — the DISPOSITION and safe-discharge plan (who admits, what gets reconciled, follow-up).
steps.rubric: Per MGH upper GI bleed: resuscitate — 2 large-bore IVs + measured crystalloid + type & cross, CAUTIOUS volume given HFpEF/Kerley B (CANT-MISS,5); transfuse pRBC to a RESTRICTIVE threshold Hgb>7 (>8 if active CAD/ischemia), watch for TACO (CANT-MISS,4); IV PPI infusion pantoprazole (3); HOLD aspirin/clopidogrel and apixaban; REVERSE apixaban (andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC) given life-threatening bleeding (CANT-MISS,4); GI consult for urgent EGD <24h (3); EMPIRIC octreotide + ceftriaxone until a variceal source is excluded at EGD (2); safe discharge/disposition — admit (ICU if unstable), reconcile meds (stop the OTC NSAID, plan when/whether to restart DAPT and apixaban with cardiology, H. pylori testing/eradication, start oral PPI), counsel on alcohol cessation, arrange GI and PCP follow-up (3). PENALTY: liberal/over-transfusion precipitating pulmonary edema (-2); restarting/continuing anticoagulation or antiplatelets during active bleeding, or discharging without med reconciliation/follow-up (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: two large-bore IVs, isotonic crystalloid in measured aliquots (his HFpEF and Kerley B lines mean cautious volume), type & cross with blood to bedside, continuous monitoring. Transfuse pRBCs to a RESTRICTIVE threshold (Hgb >7, or >8 given his active CAD), watching for transfusion-associated circulatory overload and giving furosemide between units if needed. Start an IV PPI infusion (pantoprazole). Address the bleeding diathesis: HOLD aspirin, clopidogrel, and apixaban, and REVERSE the apixaban for this life-threatening bleed (andexanet alfa, or 4-factor PCC if unavailable). Until a variceal source is excluded at endoscopy, cover EMPIRICALLY with octreotide and ceftriaxone. GI consult for urgent upper endoscopy within 24 hours (diagnostic and therapeutic). DISPOSITION / SAFE DISCHARGE once stabilized and scoped: admit (ICU if hemodynamically unstable or ongoing bleeding); medication reconciliation — STOP the over-the-counter NSAID permanently, test and treat for H. pylori, continue an oral PPI, and coordinate WITH cardiology the timing of restarting dual antiplatelet therapy and apixaban (balancing stent/stroke risk against rebleed); counsel on alcohol cessation with resources; arrange GI and primary-care follow-up and clear return precautions.

criticalActions.item: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, measured crystalloid, T&C) with cautious volume given HFpEF
Transfuse to restrictive threshold (Hgb>7, >8 if CAD), watch for TACO
Hold antiplatelets/anticoagulant and REVERSE apixaban (andexanet or 4-factor PCC)

coreActions.item: Empiric octreotide + ceftriaxone until variceal source excluded
Safe discharge/disposition: admit, med reconciliation (stop NSAID, H. pylori, restart DAPT/apixaban with cardiology), alcohol counseling, GI/PCP follow-up

bonusActions.item: Calculate a risk score (Glasgow-Blatchford) to guide disposition

penalties.item: Over-transfusion / liberal fluids precipitating pulmonary edema in HFpEF
Continuing/restarting anticoagulation or antiplatelets during active bleeding, or discharging without med reconciliation/follow-up

requiredActions: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, measured crystalloid, T&C) with cautious volume given HFpEF
Transfuse to restrictive threshold (Hgb>7, >8 if CAD), watch for TACO
Hold antiplatelets/anticoagulant and REVERSE apixaban (andexanet or 4-factor PCC)
optionalActions: Empiric octreotide + ceftriaxone until variceal source excluded
Safe discharge/disposition: admit, med reconciliation (stop NSAID, H. pylori, restart DAPT/apixaban with cardiology), alcohol counseling, GI/PCP follow-up
Calculate a risk score (Glasgow-Blatchford) to guide disposition
unsafeActions: Over-transfusion / liberal fluids precipitating pulmonary edema in HFpEF
Continuing/restarting anticoagulation or antiplatelets during active bleeding, or discharging without med reconciliation/follow-up
steps.disposition: Admit; ICU if unstable or ongoing bleeding

physicalExamMappings.finding: Vitals: BP 98/62, HR 118 (irregularly irregular), RR 20, 36.8C, SpO2 96% RA; significant orthostatic drop if checked.
HEENT: conjunctival pallor if examined; mucous membranes dry; no scleral icterus.
Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound/guarding, no distension; liver edge not enlarged; no splenomegaly.
Skin: no spider angiomata, no palmar erythema, no jaundice, no petechiae/purpura (pertinent negatives against decompensated chronic liver disease).
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: No spider angiomata, palmar erythema, or jaundice.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: no shifting dullness and no ascites; no caput medusae (argues against portal hypertension/cirrhosis).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 66M with CAD on dual antiplatelet therapy, atrial fibrillation on apixaban, HFpEF, and heavy daily alcohol use presents with melena, microcytic anemia (Hgb 7.6), an elevated BUN:Cr with a new AKI, near-syncope, and orthostatic hypotension. The diagnosis is Acute upper GI bleed, most likely an NSAID/antiplatelet peptic ulcer; the genuine competing fork is a variceal bleed from occult alcoholic cirrhosis, resolved toward a non-variceal source by normal LFTs/albumin/INR and no exam stigmata of chronic liver disease, with upper endoscopy as the discriminating test. His own EKG shows atrial fibrillation (the reason he is anticoagulated) and the chest film shows Kerley B lines that temper resuscitation in his HFpEF. Management integrates restrictive transfusion, reversal of apixaban and holding antiplatelets, IV PPI, empiric variceal cover until EGD, and a safe-discharge plan with medication reconciliation and alcohol counseling.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Vitals: BP 98/62, HR 118 (irregularly irregular), RR 20, 36.8C, SpO2 96% RA; significant orthostatic drop if checked.
HEENT: conjunctival pallor if examined; mucous membranes dry; no scleral icterus.
Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound/guarding, no distension; liver edge not enlarged; no splenomegaly.
Skin: no spider angiomata, no palmar erythema, no jaundice, no petechiae/purpura (pertinent negatives against decompensated chronic liver disease).
Abdomen: no shifting dullness and no ascites; no caput medusae (argues against portal hypertension/cirrhosis).
reasoningPathway: Frame the unstable presentation from the chart and vitals (orthostatic, irregularly irregular tachycardia, near-syncope).
Elicit the focused history, including the minimized alcohol, the OTC NSAID, and the anticoagulant/antiplatelet load only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam, eliciting orthostasis, pallor, and the pertinent NEGATIVES against chronic liver disease.
Build a differential led by an NSAID/antiplatelet peptic ulcer while keeping a variceal bleed as the must-not-miss competitor; name three drug/habit mechanisms.
Order a focused workup (including a drug-specific anti-Xa and LFTs to settle peptic-vs-variceal); read the patient's own AF on EKG and the Kerley B lines on CXR.

Commit to a non-variceal upper GI bleed with EGD as the discriminator; manage with cautious restrictive transfusion, apixaban reversal, holding antiplatelets, IV PPI, empiric variceal cover, and a safe-discharge/med-reconciliation plan with cardiology and GI follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a variceal bleed because of the alcohol history and missing the peptic/drug story
Checking only an INR and assuming the apixaban is irrelevant
Over-transfusing and precipitating pulmonary edema in a HFpEF patient
Restarting or continuing anticoagulation/antiplatelets during active bleeding
Discharging without medication reconciliation, H. pylori testing, or follow-up
managementPearls: Resuscitate with cautious volume and transfuse to a restrictive threshold, watching for TACO in HFpEF
Hold antiplatelets and reverse apixaban for a life-threatening bleed; cover empirically for varices until EGD
criticalWorkupItems: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm), no acute ST-elevation — the patient's own arrhythmia and the reason for anticoagulation.
Kerley B lines (early interstitial pulmonary edema) in a HFpEF patient — transfuse cautiously to avoid TACO.
criticalManagementItems: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, measured crystalloid, T&C) with cautious volume given HFpEF
Hold antiplatelets/anticoagulant and REVERSE apixaban (andexanet or 4-factor PCC)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm), no acute ST-elevation.
Kerley B lines (early interstitial pulmonary edema) in a HFpEF patient — transfuse cautiously to avoid TACO.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (~118 bpm), no acute ST-elevation.
Kerley B lines (early interstitial pulmonary edema) in a HFpEF patient — transfuse cautiously to avoid TACO.
imageInterpretationPearls: When the EKG is the patient's OWN study, name the rhythm and then reason about what it implies (here: AF explains the anticoagulation that is worsening the bleed).
A fast irregularly irregular rate in an anemic, hypovolemic patient is demand on top of AF — treat the bleed before the rhythm.
Subtle Kerley B lines change the resuscitation plan: correct the anemia but avoid circulatory overload.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions about alcohol and medications without judgment
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
